13 Workshop Facilitating a Value Engine Workshop - YouTube.mp4
I'm really excited. This is going to be kind of a unique session because in this session, I'm actually going to be showing you how you are going to facilitate a value engine workshop. With your team. So it's going to be a little bit meta because the slide deck that I'm actually going to be going through here, the slide deck that you can actually see right now on your screen, you have this, all right, you have this exact same slide deck and it is designed for you to be able to click through with your team to go through the exact same exercise that we're doing here. So we want to give you all the tools to go and do this, not merely the knowledge. So this is kind of the first real point of execution.
Now, just to kind of go back to where we are and where we've been and what this is really all about. Remember, our goal. Through this entire program, we want to build an operating system for your company. And an operating system is made up of, of course, a set of algorithms, a common language, these desired outputs, and the foundation. of any good operating system, the foundation of your set of algorithms, are your value engines. And so this workshop, what I'm going to be showing you how to do, what you're going to be doing with your team. Is the critical moment in this entire program. Everything that we're going to be doing for the remainder of this program is going to be built upon what happens here. So this is important.
That's why we're spending so much time on it. With that said, let's dive in and again review the pre-meeting planning and setup. All right, so let's talk about the setup to facilitate. A value engine workshop number one I'm going to really encourage you to do this in person if at all possible. You can do it remotely. I'm actually going to provide some tools and some resources if you need to do it remotely. But you're going to find that it's just a lot more effective. You can get it done in a much more timely manner if you've got everybody in the same room. It's also just a lot of fun. I think your team is going to gain a lot from this process. Speaking of your team.
For this first meeting, for the first time that you facilitate this, I would keep it as small as you can, right? So if you're a team of one, that's easy. It's just you going through this. Maybe you want to partner up with somebody else who's also in the program and do this together. But if you've got a leadership team, right. You want to make sure you've got a representative from product. You want to make sure that you've got a representative from kind of the sales and marketing, the growth side. But I'd really try to keep this to no more than five to seven people. All right. Just those core leaders. You're going to go back and review it with other members of your team. But for now. Keep it simple.
Keep it small. Keep it tight. Also, you're going to need a large room to do this in. I recommend that you have. You're gonna need like one big whiteboard like what I have here or two smaller whiteboards if at all possible because hopefully we're mapping more than just one engine and this way you can kind of keep some stuff up. Not have to erase it each and every time. Also, this is kind of optional, but if you could have a TV monitor, a stand, something so that you're— you know, the folks who are here in the meeting with you, if they could see the slides that you're reviewing, not essential, but it can be helpful just to kind of keep the pace and for folks to know what is going on.
Finally, and this is going to be a really, really important aspect of what you need. Post-it notes, right? Post-it notes are a really... Really important aspect of this you need one deck for each and every member of your team, one set of post-it notes for each and every person in the room, and a sharpie for each person in the room. So each of these, for each person in the room, got a couple of whiteboards. You're good to go. In terms of time, right? In terms of time, make sure that you're allowing at least two hours, at least two hours, at a minimum. Four hours is even better. I love the idea of starting this in the morning and going through lunch or kind of kicking it off during lunch and going to the end of the day.
So if you can give it a full half day, you're going to be able to find that you can get at least three value engines mapped if you follow the process that I'm talking about here. Also, kind of just one little side thing that we figured out having done this. It seems like a little thing. But if you are going to have a meeting that overlaps with a lunch break, order lunch ahead of time. It seems like a small thing, but we've wasted 30 minutes just trying to figure out. Where are we going to eat? What are we going to eat? What does this person want? Oh, no tomatoes for you. Again, it seems like a simple thing, but having done literally dozens of these, I can tell you, you lose a lot of time in just ordering lunch.
All right. So. If you got lunch ordered, if you got Sharpies, if you got the Post-its, if you got a big room and you got a bunch of whiteboards, you are in good shape to facilitate your value engine mapping workshop. All right, so now we are into the workshop. Itself, so I want you to imagine there's gonna be times when I'm talking directly to you and there's gonna be times I'm kind of demoing, pretending like I'm talking to people who are in the room. I'll kind of be jumping back and forth, try to follow along in general. If I'm making eye contact with you, that means I'm talking to you. If you see me sort of looking around, it means I'm pretending to talk to a room, demoing how you would do it.
Now again, the slide deck that I am going through is the exact same slide deck that you will have. And what I want to point out to you is that if you look, there's going to be a lot of presenter notes in there that will tell you exactly what to say. Also, the slides on the screen will generally model what you're supposed to say. So I think it's good to review this ahead of time. Just know when you get into it, it tends to flow pretty well. Now, I encourage you. Make this your own. So you can see here on kind of this cover slide here, we've got a spot for your logo. I would go ahead and put your company's logo in there. I would also add the date just for posterity sake.
Because it's probably going to be something that you repeat once or twice a year. All right, so go ahead and do that. Update it there. Also, we have an agenda. This is a very loose, rough agenda. If you want to modify it, tweak it, make it your own, add times, you can do that. Generally, we don't put times in because we don't want to be that definite on things, but we just kind of like to say, 'Hey, here is the overall agenda.' And then I like to kick things off by just framing. The goal for everybody in the room. So I would say it's something like this. All right, everybody, thanks so much for. Bye. Showing up today, I just want to make sure that everybody's clear on why we're doing this, right?
Our mission here is to clarify our company's value creation process, right? We want to get crystal clear and answer the question: 'How do we, as a company, generate value, right? Value for our customers and then value back for us. And so that's our goal. And what we ultimately want to do is map at least two value engines. That's it. That's it. That's essentially what you would say, kind of a secondary goal if you want to talk about this. Is encourage your team to really think about the process because, to the extent that you can embed the value engine creation process within your organization, teach your leaders to do this, they can begin doing it at the team level. And this just becomes a practice that everybody in your organization knows how to do.
All right, so we open up. Do some basic customization, go through the agenda, let everybody know how much time they're going to be in, review the goal. Once you've done that, it's time to actually get into the value engine workshop itself. So the best way to kick off the value engine workshop is to go through what we call the same page exercise. And as the name would suggest, The goal of this exercise is to get everybody in the room on the same page, agreeing, basically, on what reality is. I can't tell you how many times I've gotten leaders in the room or I've been facilitating one of these for another company. Only to discover that not everybody in the room even agreed. Who's our ideal customer? What are our top selling products?
What are the marketing campaigns that we've done in the past that actually work? It's important that everybody be on the same page and generally agreeing on what reality is if you're going to get the most out of this. So I'll tell you. What I'm about to show you is optional. This is an optional exercise, but it is highly, highly, highly recommended for all the reasons that I just showed you. showed you there. So when you get to this part in the exercise, again, kind of demoing how you would do this, right? You're going to put up these questions on the board. Now, if you have slides that are visible, you could use this exact slide. If you don't, you could simply write them on the board. You could print it out.
uh and hand it out as a worksheet however you want to do it, whatever works for you, but you would simply say, 'Okay everyone, I'm going to put ten minutes on the clock.' And what I want you to do, I'm putting ten minutes on the clock. I want everybody just to review these eight questions. and just answer them to the best of your abilities. Don't share with your neighbor, right? We're going to talk about it afterwards. So working independently, I'm going to put 10 minutes on the clock. and answer all of these. And I know that's not a lot of time. and I realized that, you know, you're going to want to work faster. It's okay. Just do the best you can. We're going to discuss it after. Okay.
10 minutes on the clock. Start with question number one. Move on down. Go ahead. That's it. All right, that is your intro. Now, I will tell you. 10 minutes to answer eight questions is not a lot of time. That's kind of the point. We just want everybody's initial gut reaction. All right, we don't want them to overthink it. We just want that initial gut reaction. Now, when the 10 minutes is over. If you want to, and this is totally your discretion, when the 10 minutes is over, you can say, 'Okay, 10 minutes are up.' Um. How many of you got through all eight questions? And if you find that only half the room got through eight questions, you can say, 'All right, what I'm going to do is give you another five minutes.' All right.
10 minutes, then give them another five minutes. Just from a facilitation standpoint, it's a really good way to keep things on track. But be a little bit gracious and give them more time. But my encouragement... Really. 10 minutes is enough time to answer all of these questions. Once you've answered these questions, all right, once you've given them kind of their 10 to 15 minutes. Then what you simply say is you go, all right, everybody. Who's willing to go first? Let's start with question number one. Who's willing to go first and say what you wrote down for question number one? And you simply go question by question. And the process is, and again, these are all in your presenter notes. So if all you do is read your presenter notes, you're going to see everything that I'm saying right now.
Who's willing to go first? Bop. What did you write for question number one? Right. Bob Gibbs. You know, I believe that our ideal customer is this type of person. Fantastic. Who agrees? And people are like, I agree. Great. You don't need a lot of discussion. Did anybody write down something different? Okay. And again, the exact— this phrase is important. That's why it's in your presenter notes. Did anyone have something different? Not do you disagree, just did you write something? different. and then have a discussion around the differences. If everybody agrees, we don't need to talk about it, move on. And the reason that this is in here where I say, 'who's willing to go first?' Great. Who agrees? Show of hands, who agrees?
I can't tell you how many times I've been in one of these workshops and we'll have a discussion for five or ten minutes and everybody in the room agrees. So if we want to keep this punchy, somebody goes first. Who agrees? Raise your hand if you agree. Great. Who wrote, who has something different? If they wrote something different, then you have a discussion about it. Very often, the differences are going to be minor. And you can point that out. Alright, it sounds like... You know, you're saying this, you're saying this, that we're close. I think we've got a good range here. We can move on. Sometimes, though, you're going to discover... That. You've got wildly opposing viewpoints on what reality is. You've got wildly opposing ideas on what is is.
All right. When that happens, you've got to devote the time to have that discussion. Now, if everybody is generally on the same page, you can get through this same page exercise in 30 minutes. Sometimes, though, this is going to go a full hour and I try not to spend more than an hour here. But if it goes a full hour because people fundamentally disagree on some of these critical core questions, right? Who is our ideal customer? Who's not our ideal customer? What will our customers always want? Right? Where are we heading, shoulders over the competition? What have we done in the past that worked? Where do you want to compete? These are really important questions for everybody. To agree on.
And so if you find there's tremendous disagreement, it is worth investing the time to get everyone on the same page, because if they're not, you're gonna find that everything else that we do, there's gonna be a lot of friction. Okay, so go ahead and invest the time. Go through the same page exercise. I think you're going to be shocked and pleasantly surprised by how much you learn and by how much your team gains from this exercise. But once you've gone through it, once everybody's generally on the same page, then you're ready to move on to the next step and actually start brainstorming and mapping out your company's value engines. All right, so now we're getting into the value engine mapping process itself. Now this process is broken up over three steps, but there's really kind of two core parts.
The first part is general brainstorming, right? We've got to do a brain dump. Uh, all the possible value engines that we could. be constructing here. Then what we need to do once we've decided on the one we're going to start with, actually go through the process of mapping. So what we want to do first is just talk about, really focus on step one. Defining the process, defining the specific engine. to be mapped. Now, when you're going through this, the question that you're going to ask everyone to answer, and that's why I have it here on the screen. And again, if this is visible, if you have a screen, I would have this up there. Where is value truly created in our business? It's just a good, big picture question to ask.
So I would say, where is value truly created in our business? Now, what I would do at this point is I would go through and I would share the Peter Drucker quote. That, if you recall, in a previous module I shared with you, right? Everything that I showed you when I taught you the value engine process you are now beginning to share that with your team as well. So going through the Drucker quote, one of the foremost business thinkers and leaders, Peter Drucker, said that there are only two things in business that make money, innovation and marketing. Everything else is just cost. So when we think about our value as a company, we're kind of going to leverage those. Add another one. These are the three types of value.
And then you would explain the three value engine types, right? Obviously, there's the growth engine, innovation. Fulfillment. Right, talk about what they are. And I would direct your attention to the presenter nodes. Because in the presenter nodes, I also give examples. These are the same examples, by the way, that I gave you in the previous session. So you say, what is it you know? So an example of a growth-driven company, like Procter & Gamble, right? They make soap and other products. A lot of their products are fairly commoditized at this point. So the value at this point in their business is less about innovation and more about just general awareness and making sure that they're on the right store shelves and in the right placements. It's about growth. Right.
Same with a company like Casper Mattress. Like, there's a bunch of different mattress companies out there. Their value is more about their ability to acquire customers. Same with you know, Uber, right? Because Uber needs to grow both in the drivers and the people who know about it. Same with gaming apps, right? You, if you want, you can use other examples that relate to your industry. Feel free to, again, adapt this and make it your own. Examples of innovation, obviously, Apple, Hollywood, fulfillment-driven companies, Amazon. Four Seasons Hotels, Eleven Madison, if you don't know, is probably one of the best restaurants in the world. And they don't, frankly, need to focus on growth. They don't really need to innovate. They've had the same menu for years. It's all about the fulfillment.
It's all about the execution. Here's a moment where I would kind of pause. And I would say. Okay, team. Obviously, we do all three. And again, the quote that I'm saying right now is in your presenter notes. Obviously, we do all three. But of the three, what is the one? [Category] where you believe that we are creating the most value as a company. All right. Allow them to discuss. Ask a brave person to go first. Who agrees? Who has something different? All right, that's your sequence. Who's willing to go first? Who agrees? Who has something different? Discuss. Now, you don't necessarily at this point have to reach consensus at this point, but it's just a good place to start to begin the brainstorming process for people to understand these categories.
Because what you're going to... Have them do next is the value engine brainstorming portion of the workshop. Now this particular um—this particular session takes about 30 minutes. Again, where you see a timer, I would actually put 30 minutes on the clock. All right, I would actually say, 'Okay, we got 30 minutes for this.' And it's broken up into these four steps. We're going to brainstorm, give everybody five minutes to do that. We're going to post and discuss. Got 10 minutes for that. We're going to kill and combine. So we're going to more discussion, more combining. I'll show you how to do that in just a second. And then we're going to prioritize and ultimately pick the one that we're going to start with and the second and third that we'll hopefully get.
During this workshop. All right. So the way this process goes, and again— This is all in your presenter notes. You would say, 'Okay, everyone. Take five minutes.' I want you to take five minutes and brainstorm as many value engines as you can. Take five minutes. brainstorm as many value engines as you can. Now remember, everyone should have their stack of post-it notes. So they're not writing these down on a sheet of paper. They'd be writing these down on post-it notes. Okay, so put five minutes on the clock. Say, go. One of the things that you're going to have to deal with as you're going through this process—is the awkward silence in the room, but it's really, really important. All right, I'm going to put five minutes on the clock.
Write as many as you can. Think about the categories, right? What are some of the ones that are more growth? What are the ones that are more innovation? What are the ones that are more kind of fulfillment? Some people might say, we don't really have a name for these necessarily. That's fine. Just generally describe them. Okay, so one value engine idea. Per post-it note, go. You got five minutes. All right, now, once you have done that, all right, so you say, okay, great, five minutes are up. Now what I want to do is I want to post and discuss. Um, who's willing to go first? Who wrote down a lot of things? And what I like doing is looking around the room. And I like seeing.
um, I like looking around the room and seeing who's got the biggest stack. who wrote the most stuff down generally have them go first. Okay. And you say, okay, Bob, looks like you've got a lot of stuff written down. So Bob, why don't you come, come up to the whiteboard. and I want you to to post yours and I want you to put them in the category that you believe they fall in. So this. Think this, this three-column grid is what I would want you to write. So you've got a column for growth, a column for innovation, and a column for fulfillment. Okay? Growth, innovation, fulfillment. So Bob comes up and Bob goes, okay. We've got the process here that we use. This is kind of more of a growth one.
This is how we acquire new leads from an innovation. We do our, you know, kind of our innovation sprint planning agile process. So that's kind of here. We've also got the process once somebody's in, how we sell to them. That's kind of over here. And, you know, really from a fulfillment process. We need to fulfill this product over here. and we need to fulfill this one over here. Okay. So Bob comes up and does that. Then what you say. All right, who's willing to go next? And they would go back and they'd repeat the process, right? Okay. I've got. And you're going to find that your product or your services team, they've got more on the innovation. You know, and they're going to have a bit more, obviously.
And when it comes to the fulfillment, side. That's going to be great. There's also going to be some times where somebody goes, Oh, you know what? I had that same one. I wrote down the same thing. Great! Go ahead and start. All right, so just right here how it went. Stack it. If they wrote down the same thing, stack it, we're beginning the process of combining. All right, so over here, same thing, innovation. And maybe this one is kind of closely related, so I'll put it in here. All right. That is the second stage, which is post and discuss. So each person, they talk about what they wrote down, they post it up there. Once you're done with that, and again, I would expect no more than about 10 to 15 minutes on that step, depending on the number of people who are in the room.
It actually does move faster, especially when there's duplicate, because you don't need to re-discuss and re-explain it. But the next step is the kill and combine. All right, kill and combine. So maybe when you're looking at it, you're saying. Really? You know what? This innovation one over here. It's exactly like this one here, so we're going to combine those. And maybe you come over here and you go, 'This fulfillment one, this isn't really so much fulfillment. This isn't super relevant.' Let's go ahead and Just knock that one off. All right, so we're going to kill. We're going to combine. And then once you're done with that, you simply say, 'Okay.' Out of all of the possible value engines, all the possible like critical core value creation processes that we put up here on this board.
What is the one? All right, what's the one that we all believe we should start with? So when you get to the prioritize stage, you're simply going to say, 'Which value engine do we believe we should map first?' And this is really important because you're not saying, 'what's the only one we're going to do?' You're simply saying, 'what's the one that we're going to do first?' And perhaps you say before. You know, it seemed like we all agreed that fulfillment was where we create value. So maybe we start within this category. All right. and I would recommend that you basically pick one, two, and three. And once you've done that, right, you've done your brainstorming. You've done your post and discuss, you have killed and combined the ones that are similar, and you have ultimately prioritized.
Once you've done that, you're ready to move on to step two. All right, so now that we have prioritized and we know which value engine we're going to map first, we're going to get into actually defining what is the beginning, what is the end. In other words, step two, we need to define the triggering and ending events. And the question that you're going to ask, again, I would have this question actually pulled up on the screen so that people could see it, they could continue to think about it, right? What is the triggering event? That initiates this process and what is the final action that concludes it. In other words, what you say is, 'Okay, we all agree that this is the value engine that we're going to map, so the question is, where does it start?' All right, where does it start?
And then I would have everybody think and go, 'Okay, who believes they know where it starts?' Okay, who believes I know where to start? Okay, what do you think, Bob? I think it starts here. Okay. Who agrees? Who disagrees? Does anybody have something different? You would be shocked. And how... Often. In a room of very intelligent people, they don't even necessarily all agree on where the process begins. But once it is clear and everybody said, 'all right.' Everybody agrees this is where it starts? Great. Just simply put your very first post-it note, write down what that first stage is, put that first post-it note right there under 'start.' We're not going to draw a whole lot of lines yet. All right, we'll get to that.
All right, we all agree that this is the start of our particular process. Where is it? Where are we going to declare that this particular process has ended? Now, this is a really good time to remind them that the final action of one. Value engine is very often the starting point of another. All right, so you can give the example. And again, all of this is in your presenter notes. Typically, a growth engine right will end. Right, a growth engine typically will end and that will begin a fulfillment. engine. Typically, not always, but typically. So it's a good thing to remind them because this can also inform which which value engine do we want to map next? All right, so everybody at this point says. This is the end.
You'd go through the same process. Who thinks they know where it ends? Who agrees? Who disagrees? Let's go. Once everybody in the room agrees on, this is the beginning. This is the end. Once you have that in place and only once you are agreeing on the full scope of this particular value engine, then you're ready to move on to the next step. All right, so we are now into step three, which is really my favorite part of this process. This is when we're going to brainstorm all the tasks and activities, all the little steps that are going to take us from the starting point to the ending point. We're basically going to fill in the middle, fill in the gap. And the question that you're going to ask. Of your team here.
Is simply, and then what happens. All right, and the way that I like to do this, and again, this exact. Whoa. That I'm going to say is in your presenter notes, right? So I would say it just like this. Okay. So we all agree that. This is where it starts. And this is where it ends. So the question that I have for all of you is, if this is the start, then what? Okay, if this is the start, we all agree. Then what happened? And again, I would allow it to kind of popcorn around the room. What's the next one? What's the next one? That's where you go, okay? Got it? We all agree? Okay. This one? Everybody agree with that? Okay, great. This is the next, then what?
All right, then what? And that's when you're writing down, right? So you've got your deck of post notes. You're writing down what it is. You say, 'Okay, cool.' All right, great. Then what? Okay? Then what? Now, what you're going to find as you're going through this process is that oftentimes... it's going to be another step. Just this is another step in the process. Sometimes you're going to find that what actually happens is somebody needs to approve. Somebody needs to review, to audit. And say, 'Yes, we're clear to move forward.' Sometimes you're going to come do a step and you're going to hear the word, it depends. So you're going to get here and say, 'Okay, then what happens again?' Well, it depends. Did they buy or not buy?
Did they opt in or not opt in? Um... Did the person... You know, were they able to get it installed or not? If you hear an 'it depends.' If you were at some kind of a gateway. Right? Then you know what to do. Subtitles by the Amara . org community. If you've gone through the training, and this is an important thing, I've got this slide in here if you want to share this. With your team, talk about kind of the different shapes. But what you would simply do is say, 'Okay, we've got an it depends.' So what we need here is: I'm going to turn this into a decision diamond. I'm going to go through. And you know, as you can see here, theme. This one.
We've just transformed the square into a diamond. Now, maybe you put it here. Now, the exact order in which things go is generally irrelevant at this point. We're going to connect the dots and we're going to connect them later. I'm going to say this again. Do not connect dots. Do not draw arrows just yet. Because what you're going to find at some point is somebody goes, oh, you know what? Oh my gosh, I just realized we forgot an important step. Right. Really. This is. First, we got to do this one. All right. So you kind of need to move them around. When you're going through the process, right? You're going through and you're moving them around. Then, after you feel like you've got a general flow, you can say, 'Okay, this goes to here.' This one goes to here.
This one drops down. Then this one goes here. And you can connect the dots later on. All right. That's, that's kind of how. How that process works as you go through it. But once you've gone through this entire process, right? You said. This is where it begins. This is where it ends. You said, and then what? And then what? And then what? Once you've gone through the entire process and you have connected the dots, all the way to the end stage. At that point, congratulations, you have mapped your very first value engine. All right, so once you have completed and done one of these, gone through this process here, it should look something like. This. It should look something like what you see here. If you find that...
You're going on to a million different whiteboards and things are kind of all over the place. It's possible that what you really have are multiple value engines. So when you're done, it should look something a bit like this, right? Once you have this, then I recommend that you take a picture. All right, take a picture. And once you have that picture, once you have that more or less documented, then erase the board. And repeat the process. Over again, and let's see if, during this value mapping workshop, if you can't get at least two of them done. Ideally, you can get three of them done. This whole process that I just showed you, once you did the initial brainstorming, that's step one. Steps two and three. Generally take between 30 and 60 minutes.
All right— 30 and 60 minutes. So, if you figure, if you got four hours, if you if you set aside an entire half day to do this. There's really no reason, even with the earlier same page exercise, that you shouldn't be able to get at least two of them done. Ideally, three. Maybe even four. All right. So that's kind of your goal. That's your objective. Now, once you have done that, you've completed these, you've got these documented, you've taken some pictures so that when it gets erased or if these things, post-it notes fall off the board, you haven't lost your work. Once you've done that, then you're ready to proceed on to some of the next steps to begin the rollout process of this throughout the organization.
So that's what we're going to do next. So congratulations, what you have now completed, if you've gone through the full value engine workshop, is you have mapped at least two, hopefully three, maybe even more value engines. For your particular company. Now we need to begin the process of rolling this out through the organization. And to do this, there are a couple of critical next steps that you're going to want to go through. The first is the stakeholder review. So what you would simply do is you would say to your team, 'Okay, everybody. You know, and I'm not talking to everybody in the room, right? Okay, everybody, so we have mapped, let's say, three value engines. We mapped a growth, we mapped an innovation, and we mapped a fulfillment.
Or maybe we mapped two growths and a fulfillment, right? Maybe you didn't get to an innovation— one, that's fine. But we've mapped three of the most— critical value engines within our business. Now what I want you to do is: I want you to go to your team. So if you're talking to your managers, they could go and do a stakeholder review with their team. Or if you're a small enough company, perhaps you bring everybody together in an all-hand style meeting. And the question that drives this meeting— you put up, you know— whether it's... An image or something like that, you go ahead and share the. Here's what we did. And maybe it's still on the whiteboard, right? Maybe it's still the whiteboard. You actually bring everybody in the same room, but you ask this question.
What are you currently doing? That isn't represented on this flow chart. That's the question. What are you currently doing that is not represented on this flowchart? That's what you ask of the people in the room. Now, invariably, when they go through, you're going to find some people say, 'Well, I'm doing this thing over here.' and it's totally unrelated to the stuff on the flowchart that's fine; it probably is going to show up on a different value engine. Right. Maybe you discover that some of the work that somebody is doing isn't even necessary. And so you can reallocate their time, but it's mostly there. To get kind of one last check. Because you want to find out, did we miss a step? Is there a critical aspect of the process that you missed?
Now, more times than not, when they say something, you're going to find that, oh, what you just said, it actually exists right here. That thing you described, I think it's a piece of this process here. That's great. That's really valuable, right? Because it gives them context into how the job they do, how their work fits within this, which fits within a broader value creation process. It's hugely, hugely valuable. But if nothing else, this is an opportunity to kind of have just like one last little check. Point to see: did we get them? Did we get them all? It's also really, really good for buying. Then, what I want you to do while you're doing this stakeholder review. And so, I want you to say: 'Okay.
Out of all of the steps that we mapped in this particular process. What are the ones that are truly critical? Right. What are the ones that if we mess this up? Right? Nothing else matters.' Or what are the ones that, if we really get this right, we can mess up some other stuff and it's going to be just fine. These are called power stages. So talk it over with the team and identify what are the power stages. I would simply just— you know— have a little sharpie or whatever, just go over and say, 'Okay, well, obviously this one is a power stage.' And you know, we're also going to say that, you know, that this step right here where we did this audit. That's another really important power stage, okay?
We're just gonna denote. Not every step is of equal importance. All of them are necessary, but some of them are more important than others. What are the ones that are particularly critical? Which ones are most critical? Identify those now. You'll see why we're going to do that when we get into. Some later modules and some later sessions. Then, kind of the last step, once everybody's agreed, you've got the sign-off, you've agreed on what the power stages are, the last step is to publish your value engine. Now, there are a lot of different tools that you can use to do this, whether it's a paid tool like Lucidchart or Gliffy or a free tool like Draw. io. Find the tool that's best for you, but once everybody has agreed, everybody signed off.
That's when you can actually document it in a much prettier form, okay? Once you have gone through these steps, then you have officially you've officially documented and rolled out one of your company's value engines. So just repeat this process for all your different value engines. And you're going to find that once you do this, everybody in your organization is much clearer on how the work that they do fits within the broader value creation process. And most importantly, once you've documented your value creation process, you can begin optimizing your value creation process. And that is what having a scalable operating system is all about.
